# 1 Samuel 10: The King God Gave, and the King Israel Demanded

Samuel anoints Saul with oil in private, and God immediately begins to transform him—changing his heart, filling him with the Spirit, enabling him to prophesy among the prophets. Yet notice what Saul does with this extraordinary encounter: he keeps it secret. When his uncle asks what Samuel said, Saul mentions the donkeys but refuses to speak of the kingship. This is the first crack in Saul's character. God has anointed him publicly before Heaven, given him signs, and touched him with the Holy Spirit, and Saul responds by hiding the word of the Lord. He has been chosen, empowered, and affirmed—and his instinct is to conceal it. There is something deeply unsettling in this silence. It suggests that even in the moment of his calling, Saul is uncertain, perhaps even ashamed. He does not trust that what God has done in him is enough.

When Samuel gathers all Israel at Mizpah, he confronts them with a brutal truth: they have rejected God. The Lord rescued them from Egypt, delivered them from oppressors, and gave them salvation and rest. In return, they demanded a king—a visible, earthly ruler they could see and touch. Samuel does not soften this indictment. He calls it what it is: rejection. But God, in His sovereignty, grants what His people have demanded. Through the casting of lots, Saul is selected. Yet when the moment comes to present him, Saul is hiding among the baggage. The very man God has chosen to lead refuses to be seen. Israel demanded a king, and God gave them one—but this king will not even step forward to claim his throne until he is dragged out.

When Saul finally stands before the people, he is physically impressive: taller than anyone else, striking in appearance. Samuel points him out with words of divine endorsement, and the people shout their approval. But something is fundamentally wrong. Israel got the king they wanted—strong, tall, impressive—yet Samuel has already warned them what kingship would cost (see 1 Samuel 8). And Saul, though anointed and empowered by God's Spirit, shows signs of weakness where it matters most: he will not speak of what God has done, he hides when called to step forward, and he remains silent even when despised and rejected by worthless men. God did not fail in this appointment. But He gave Israel a king who would prove to be a mirror of their own unfaith—a leader who would not truly trust the God who called him, who would seek security in his own strength, and who would ultimately turn away from the One who anointed him.
